Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of just wing it whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Sometimes I'd sit down with good intentions but end up scrolling through my phone or getting distracted by other stuff. I know I should have been more organized, but motivation was just really hard to find most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly cram when a deadline was breathing down my neck. It depended a lot on what else was going on in my life and whether I had the energy to care that day. Regular times? That wasn't really my thing this semester.
Q3: Usually it was either because I got a notification about something due or because one of my classmates mentioned something in our group chat. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which was kind of overwhelming honestly. The peer support group I was part of would remind me too, which helped push me to at least check in.
Q4: For a little while, yeah, logging in became more normal when I was connecting with my peer group regularly. They'd message me about discussions or assignments and I'd feel like I should at least show up. But I wouldn't say it ever felt truly automatic for me—it was more like social pressure keeping me somewhat accountable.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually pretty helpful when I actually participated. Hearing how other students explained things made more sense than the lectures sometimes. The videos were okay too, I watched a lot of them, but I'd zone out halfway through pretty often. Reading materials I could go at my own pace with felt more manageable.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the extra readings and optional practice stuff. It just felt like too much when I was already struggling to keep up with the required work. If it wasn't going to be graded, my brain just couldn't prioritize it. I know that probably hurt me on exams, but in the moment it felt like survival mode.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd actually do it. Also, if it was something my peers were talking about or working on together, that made it feel more worth my time. Stuff that felt abstract or disconnected from anything concrete? I just couldn't bring myself to care about it.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them, like I had to do them even if I didn't want to. Optional things? My brain just filed them under 'nice to have but probably not gonna happen.' The pressure wasn't there, so neither was my effort, unfortunately.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download readings or take screenshots of stuff and then study from those later, away from my laptop. Sometimes I'd work through things in my head while doing other stuff, like walking or whatever. Not everything I did showed up in the system because I wasn't always logged in while doing it.
Q10: There were times I'd print out notes or work through problems on paper without the LMS knowing. Or I'd reread downloaded PDFs on my phone during breaks at my part-time job. I also talked through concepts with friends from the peer support group in person, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere online.
Q11: Yeah, for sure. I'd open a video or document once, download it or screenshot the important parts, and then never open it on the LMS again. It was just easier to have stuff saved locally so I didn't have to keep logging in. The system probably thought I only looked at it once, but I went back to it multiple times.
Q12: Around midterms everything just crashed for me. I was stressed about money, working extra shifts, and the coursework started piling up in a way that felt impossible. My motivation tanked and I just felt like I was drowning. Even logging in felt exhausting during those weeks.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired from work or stressed about bills, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Sometimes I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely pulled my attention away from the course more than I'd like to admit.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but be thinking about something else entirely. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually processing the words. I was technically 'engaged' according to the system, but mentally I was checked out. It's frustrating because I know the time was wasted.
Q15: I put off the bigger assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. It was like this weird paralysis where I knew I needed to do it but couldn't make myself start. The anxiety of starting felt worse than the anxiety of not doing it, which makes no sense but that's how it was.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore stuff for weeks and then panic-work the night before. The LMS would probably show these huge spikes in my activity right before due dates. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could make myself actually do things.
Q17: When I got feedback, it was kind of a mixed bag. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I could do this, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid the course entirely. It's weird—I wanted feedback but also dreaded it. After getting my exam score back, I honestly just felt defeated.
Q18: The course structure was pretty overwhelming with eight online courses going at once. There was so much content and I never felt like I could catch up. Having the peer support helped me figure out what to prioritize, but the sheer amount of stuff made it hard to stay on top of everything.
Q19: The weekly modules were helpful for knowing what to focus on, but there was just too much material packed in. Clearer guidance on what was most important would have helped me a lot. Also, some of the assignment instructions were confusing, which made me procrastinate more because I didn't know where to start.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I'd log in, watch videos, browse discussions, but then choke when it came to actually submitting work. Sometimes I understood the material okay but couldn't translate that into completing assignments. Fear of failure or just being overwhelmed can make you active but unproductive.
Q21: I could see how that happens if someone studies mostly offline or already knows the material. That wasn't really me though—I needed more engagement, not less. But I get it, some people just work differently and don't need to be logged in constantly to learn.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. It's just a partial picture.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real effort. It's not just logging in or watching videos—it's thinking about the material, asking questions, trying to understand. I wanted to be engaged, but my motivation and stress levels made it really hard to get there.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling mentally or how much my financial stress affected my focus. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that actually kept me going or the times I studied offline. It also can't show that I actually wanted to do better—I just couldn't figure out how to make it happen.
